title: CS50 2D - Lecture 0 - Pong
summary: CS50 2D is Harvard's introductory course on 2D game development fundamentals using Lua and Love2D. The first lecture covers Pong, teaching game loops, input handling, collision detection, object-oriented programming, game states, and sound integration.
writeup: CS 50's introduction to 2D game development opens with Pong, using Lua and the Love2D framework to teach the core primitives every 2D game requires: delta-time movement, AABB collision, state management, object-oriented game objects, and sound. The course arc runs from Pong through Flappy Bird, Mario, and Pokemon, chosen because those fundamentals in Pong compose into all subsequent games. Love2D is positioned not as a toy but as a production-capable framework, with the commercially successful card game Balatro (released 2024) cited as evidence.
key_points: Love2D is a free, open-source C++ framework scripted in Lua that provides graphics, input, audio, and math modules for 2D games, and ships Lua bundled so no separate Lua install is needed.

Lua is a lightweight, dynamically typed language syntactically similar to JavaScript, uses tables (analogous to JS objects) as its universal data structure, and is widely used for scripting in commercial game engines.

Pong encapsulates the full set of 2D game fundamentals: movable rectangles, ball-paddle collision detection, score state, win conditions, and sound effects, making it the complete foundation for every later game in the course.
Delta time is introduced as the mechanism for frame-rate-independent movement, a prerequisite for any real-time game where objects must move consistently regardless of CPU speed.
Balatro, a commercially released and commercially successful card roguelike (2024), is written in Love2D, demonstrating the framework is viable for shipped, non-prototype games.
The course structures Pong as an incremental build, starting from a blank file and reaching a full implementation step by step, with all source code released publicly.
